items: Immediate Separation: Put each cat in their own room with food, water, litter box, and a familiar-smelling item, avoiding rooms next to each other to prevent stress from proximity.
Immediate Separation: Check both cats carefully for injuries around the neck, face, and base of the tail, and schedule a vet visit if you find blood, swelling, or flinching.
Immediate Separation: Observe which cat appears more frightened and withdrawn to determine who was likely the target versus the initiator of the aggression.

Immediate Separation: Spend 15 minutes sitting quietly with each cat separately, speaking softly and watching for defensive body language including tight posture, dilated pupils, or a twitching tail tip.
Medical Assessment: Look for pre-fight personality changes in the aggressor like decreased interest in food, increased withdrawal, or unusual irritability that suggests pain-referral aggression.
Medical Assessment: Determine if the aggression was disproportionate to the trigger, meaning a normal approach from the other cat set off an extreme reaction that doesn't fit the situation.
Medical Assessment: Note whether the aggressor's behavior is inconsistent, being fine with you but hostile toward the other cat or behaving differently in different rooms.
Medical Assessment: Schedule a veterinary workup as your priority if pain-referral patterns are present, because medical intervention takes precedence over behavioral reintroduction.
Root Cause Mapping: Reconstruct the three days before the fight by going day by day and noting any guests, furniture changes, new household items, or routine disruptions.
Root Cause Mapping: Write down any changes to your own schedule in the past week, including work hour changes, overnight absences, or increased visitor frequency.
Root Cause Mapping: Consider whether the fight was the final event in a stress cascade rather than a standalone incident, checking for accumulated low-level triggers you may have missed.
Root Cause Mapping: Document all environmental changes and cat behaviors you observe to identify the specific domino that fell last and triggered the fight.
